title: Add UDP protocol simulation with TCP vs UDP comparison mode
description: UDP is missing entirely — cited as essential by ALL 6 academic sources (MIT, Stanford, CMU, Berkeley, Kurose, Tanenbaum) and ALL interview guides. Cannot teach TCP vs UDP tradeoffs without it. Add connectionless send/receive simulation showing fire-and-forget delivery, packet loss without retransmission, no ordering guarantee. Include side-by-side comparison mode: same data sent via TCP and UDP simultaneously, showing the reliability/speed tradeoff visually.
acceptanceCriteria: UDP engine produces events showing connectionless send/receive with no handshake
Visualization shows packets dropped/reordered without retransmission
TCP vs UDP side-by-side mode sends same payload through both, showing timing difference
Step descriptions explain WHY UDP is used (gaming, streaming, DNS queries, VoIP)
Deep-dive data added to protocol-deep-dive.ts with header fields and use cases

title: Add IP addressing, subnetting, and CIDR interactive calculator
description: IP addressing and subnetting are CORE academic topics (all 6 sources) and the #1 student pain point (Cisco forums, CompTIA). Students struggle because it requires binary math and the notation is confusing. Add a step-by-step visual subnetting calculator that builds from 4-bit examples up to full /24 CIDR, with animated binary-to-decimal conversion. Cover IPv4 classes, CIDR notation, subnet masks, network/broadcast addresses, and available host ranges.
acceptanceCriteria: Interactive subnet calculator accepts IP + CIDR and shows binary breakdown
Step-by-step animation shows how subnet mask divides network/host portions
Covers IPv4 class A/B/C, CIDR notation, private IP ranges (10.x, 172.16.x, 192.168.x)
Visual binary-to-decimal conversion for each octet
Practice mode: given a CIDR, calculate network address, broadcast, host range

title: Add TCP congestion control visualization (slow start, AIMD, cubic, BBR)
description: TCP congestion control is CORE in all 6 academic sources (entire weeks dedicated to it at Stanford CS144 and Berkeley CS168) and the #3 student pain point. BBR is now textbook-standard (Tanenbaum 6e). Add animated visualization of congestion window over time: slow start exponential growth → congestion avoidance linear growth → packet loss → window halving. Show AIMD, TCP Reno, Cubic, and BBR side-by-side. Throughput graph updates in real time.
acceptanceCriteria: Congestion window graph animates over time showing slow start and congestion avoidance phases
Packet loss events trigger visible window reduction (AIMD halving)
Side-by-side comparison of Reno vs Cubic vs BBR on same network conditions
Step descriptions explain each phase transition and WHY
Configurable network parameters: RTT, bandwidth, loss rate

title: Add OSI model interactive layer-by-layer packet encapsulation visualizer
description: The OSI model is the #2 student pain point ('confusing as hell' — AnandTech forums) and CORE in all academic sources. Students cannot distinguish layers because real protocols blend them. Add an interactive visualization where typing a URL triggers encapsulation: Application → Transport → Network → Data Link → Physical. At each layer, show the header prepended, size growing, and addressing changing. Then decapsulate at the server end. Map real protocols to each layer.
acceptanceCriteria: User types a URL and watches data get encapsulated layer by layer
Each layer clearly shows header being prepended with byte sizes
Real protocols mapped at each layer (HTTP, TCP, IP, Ethernet, physical)
Decapsulation shown at destination (reverse process)
Comparison mode: OSI 7-layer vs TCP/IP 4-layer models side by side

title: Add routing algorithms visualization (link-state, distance-vector, BGP, OSPF)
description: Routing is CORE in all 6 academic sources (dedicated weeks at MIT, CMU, Berkeley) and appears in network engineer interviews. Currently completely missing. Add interactive graph where nodes are routers, edges have weights (latency/cost). Visualize: (1) Distance-vector (Bellman-Ford, split horizon, counting-to-infinity), (2) Link-state (Dijkstra, LSA flooding), (3) OSPF (intra-domain, area hierarchy), (4) BGP (inter-AS path selection, policy routing). BGP is the #8 student pain point.
acceptanceCriteria: Interactive network graph with configurable topology (add/remove routers and links)
Distance-vector: step-by-step routing table convergence with Bellman-Ford
Link-state: Dijkstra SPF computation with LSA flooding animation
BGP: autonomous system view with path advertisement and selection
Shows counting-to-infinity problem and split horizon solution

title: Add load balancing simulation (L4 vs L7, algorithms, health checks)
description: Load balancing is CORE in ALL interview sources (HelloInterview, ByteByteGo, NeetCode, Grokking, SystemDesignPrimer, DarkInterview) and #2 professional essential topic. Add interactive simulation showing: L4 (transport-level, IP/port routing) vs L7 (application-level, URL/header routing). Algorithms: round-robin, weighted round-robin, least connections, IP hash, consistent hashing. Show health check probes and backend server failure/recovery.
acceptanceCriteria: L4 vs L7 comparison mode showing what each layer can inspect
5+ load balancing algorithms visualized with animated request distribution
Health check probes visible with server failure/recovery scenarios
Request distribution histogram showing algorithm fairness
Client-side vs server-side load balancing comparison

title: Add 'What Happens When You Type a URL' capstone end-to-end flow
description: This is the single most commonly asked networking interview question at ALL levels (GitHub alex/what-happens-when, Glassdoor, HelloInterview). It tests the entire networking stack. Add an interactive capstone that traces a URL request through: (1) URL parsing, (2) DNS resolution, (3) ARP, (4) TCP handshake, (5) TLS handshake, (6) HTTP request/response, (7) Server processing, (8) Browser rendering, (9) Connection teardown. Each step links to its dedicated simulation. The depth of response reveals seniority — show beginner/intermediate/advanced detail levels.
acceptanceCriteria: End-to-end animated flow from URL to rendered page with 9+ stages
Each stage clickable to jump to its dedicated protocol simulation
3 detail levels: beginner (high-level), intermediate (protocol details), advanced (byte-level)
Timing breakdown showing which stage takes the most time
Works as a capstone review exercise linking all other protocol simulations

title: Add ARP (Address Resolution Protocol) visualization
description: ARP is COMMON in academic sources (Berkeley CS168, Kurose 8e, Tanenbaum 6e) and required for the 'What Happens When' capstone. It bridges Layer 2 (MAC) and Layer 3 (IP). Add visualization showing ARP request broadcast, ARP reply unicast, ARP table caching, and gratuitous ARP. Beautiful broadcast animation opportunity — a packet asks 'Who has this IP?' and all devices hear it.
acceptanceCriteria: ARP request broadcast animation showing all devices on subnet receiving the request
ARP reply unicast from matching device
ARP table cache visualization with TTL expiry
Gratuitous ARP scenario (IP conflict detection)
Step descriptions explain MAC-to-IP mapping purpose

title: Add DHCP (Discover-Offer-Request-Acknowledge) handshake simulation
description: DHCP is COMMON in academic sources (Berkeley CS168, Kurose 8e, Tanenbaum 6e) and covered by HelloInterview. The 4-step DORA handshake is a perfect fit for the existing SequenceDiagram component. Fills the Layer 3 gap. Add visualization of DHCP Discover (broadcast), Offer (unicast from server), Request (broadcast confirmation), Acknowledge (unicast with lease). Show IP lease time, renewal process, and relay agent for cross-subnet operation.
acceptanceCriteria: DHCP engine produces 4+ events for the DORA flow
Sequence diagram shows Client, DHCP Server with broadcast/unicast indication
IP address lease with TTL countdown visible
Renewal process (T1/T2 timers) shown as follow-up scenario
Step descriptions explain each phase and IP assignment mechanics

title: Add NAT (Network Address Translation) visualization
description: NAT is CORE academic (CMU, Berkeley, Kurose, Tanenbaum) and appears in interview sources. The #7 student pain point (NAT traversal). Every home network uses NAT. Add visualization of: Static NAT (1:1 mapping), Dynamic NAT (pool), PAT/NAPT (port-based, most common). Show NAT translation table, port mapping, and why NAT breaks end-to-end connectivity. Bridge to WebRTC STUN/TURN explanation.
acceptanceCriteria: Visualization shows private IP → public IP translation with port mapping
NAT translation table visible and updating in real-time
3 NAT types demonstrated: static, dynamic, PAT
Shows why NAT breaks incoming connections (no reverse mapping)
Links to STUN/TURN concepts for NAT traversal

title: Add SSE and Long Polling simulations to complete real-time protocol spectrum
description: ByteByteGo, DarkInterview, and Hemaks all cover long polling vs SSE vs WebSocket as a comparison topic. Interview candidates must differentiate all real-time options. Add: (1) Long Polling engine (request → server holds → event arrives → respond → client reconnects), (2) SSE engine (single HTTP connection, server pushes events, EventSource API). Show side-by-side: Long Polling vs SSE vs WebSocket vs HTTP polling — same data stream through all 4.
acceptanceCriteria: Long Polling engine simulates hold-and-release pattern with reconnection
SSE engine simulates unidirectional server push with event stream
4-way comparison: HTTP polling vs Long Polling vs SSE vs WebSocket on same data
Descriptions explain trade-offs: simplicity vs latency vs bandwidth
Decision guide: 'Use SSE when..., Use WebSocket when..., Use Long Polling when...'

title: Add QUIC standalone protocol simulation (beyond HTTP/3 mention)
description: QUIC is now textbook-standard (Tanenbaum 6e chapter on TCP/QUIC/BBR, Kurose 8e). Currently only mentioned in HTTP/3 comparison. ~35.8% of websites advertise HTTP/3 over QUIC. Needs its own deep-dive: connection establishment (1-RTT, 0-RTT), connection migration (IP change survival), independent stream multiplexing (no HOL blocking), integrated TLS 1.3. Compare directly with TCP.
acceptanceCriteria: QUIC engine simulates connection setup (1-RTT new, 0-RTT resumption)
Connection migration demo: show client changing IP without dropping connection
Independent stream visualization: packet loss on stream A doesn't block stream B
Integrated TLS comparison: QUIC vs TCP+TLS handshake step count
Deep-dive data added to protocol-deep-dive.ts

title: Add reverse proxy and API gateway comparison visualization
description: Reverse proxy and API gateway are CORE interview topics (ByteByteGo, DarkInterview, SystemDesignPrimer). Add visualization showing: forward proxy vs reverse proxy vs API gateway. Demonstrate request routing, SSL termination, load balancing, rate limiting, authentication, and request transformation. Show when to use Nginx (reverse proxy) vs Kong/API Gateway vs direct service-to-service.
acceptanceCriteria: Forward proxy vs reverse proxy vs API gateway comparison diagram
Request flow through each showing which features apply at each layer
SSL termination visualization
Rate limiting and authentication at API gateway level
Decision guide: 'Use reverse proxy when..., Use API gateway when...'

title: Add WebRTC ICE/STUN/TURN NAT traversal visualization
description: WebRTC is SPECIALIZED in interviews but critical for video/audio system design questions. NAT traversal is the #7 student pain point. Add: ICE candidate gathering (host, server-reflexive, relay), STUN hole-punching animation, TURN relay fallback. Show why ~30% of WebRTC sessions need TURN relay. Demonstrate Trickle ICE for faster connection.
acceptanceCriteria: ICE candidate types visualized: host, srflx, relay
STUN request/response showing public IP discovery
NAT hole-punching animation showing successful P2P connection
TURN relay fallback when direct connection fails
Statistics: percentage of connections needing each path

title: Add MQTT simulation (data exists in deep-dive, no interactive viz)
description: MQTT has detailed deep-dive data in protocol-deep-dive.ts (6 header fields, 4 handshake steps, performance, use cases, tradeoffs) but no interactive simulation. MQTT is the dominant IoT protocol and appears in Educative's networking curriculum. Add: CONNECT/CONNACK handshake, PUBLISH/SUBSCRIBE with topic filtering, 3 QoS levels (0/1/2) side-by-side comparison, retained messages, last will and testament.
acceptanceCriteria: MQTT broker/client simulation with CONNECT/CONNACK handshake
Topic-based pub/sub with wildcard topic filters (+, #)
QoS 0 vs 1 vs 2 side-by-side showing message delivery guarantees
Retained message and last will scenarios
Sequence diagram showing broker routing messages to subscribers

title: Add Ethernet and switching (MAC addresses, VLANs, STP) visualization
description: Ethernet/LANs are CORE academic topics (5 of 6 sources). VLANs appear in CMU and Tanenbaum. Add: MAC address format, Ethernet frame structure, switch MAC table learning, VLAN tagging (802.1Q), Spanning Tree Protocol (loop prevention). Bridges the gap between IP networking and physical infrastructure.
acceptanceCriteria: Ethernet frame structure with field breakdown
Switch MAC address table learning animation (frame → learn → forward)
VLAN tagging showing traffic isolation between VLANs
STP root bridge election and loop prevention
Comparison: hub vs switch vs router at each OSI layer

title: Add Zero Trust vs perimeter security comparison visualization
description: 81% of organizations plan to adopt Zero Trust by 2026 (CIO). 65% replacing VPNs with ZTNA. NIST SP 800-207 defines the framework. Add: traditional perimeter model (castle-and-moat) vs Zero Trust (verify everything). Show identity verification at every access point, micro-segmentation, least-privilege access. Compare VPN tunnel vs ZTNA per-app access.
acceptanceCriteria: Side-by-side: perimeter security (firewall boundary) vs Zero Trust (verify every request)
Request flow showing identity → device posture → policy → access decision
VPN vs ZTNA comparison for remote access
Micro-segmentation visualization showing lateral movement prevention
Reference NIST SP 800-207 framework

title: Add SDN (Software-Defined Networking) overview visualization
description: SDN is now textbook-standard (Tanenbaum 6e dedicated section, Berkeley CS168 2 weeks, Kurose 8e Ch 5). No longer niche. Add: control plane / data plane separation, OpenFlow match-action rules, centralized SDN controller, programmable forwarding tables. Show how SDN enables network automation and policy-based routing.
acceptanceCriteria: Control plane vs data plane separation clearly visualized
SDN controller sending flow rules to switches
OpenFlow match-action table with configurable rules
Comparison: traditional distributed routing vs centralized SDN
Real-world examples: Google B4, datacenter networking

title: Add DNS over HTTPS (DoH) vs DoT vs traditional DNS comparison
description: DoH is mainstream (all browsers default). Privacy-first DNS is a 2025-2026 trend. Add comparison: traditional plaintext DNS (port 53/UDP) vs DNS over TLS (DoT, port 853) vs DNS over HTTPS (DoH, port 443). Show encryption envelope, privacy implications, and performance overhead. Include DNSSEC for authentication.
acceptanceCriteria: 3-way comparison: plaintext DNS vs DoT vs DoH with packet visualization
Encryption envelope visible for DoT and DoH
Privacy comparison: what each reveals to network observers
Performance overhead comparison (connection setup time)
DNSSEC validation chain visualization

title: Add IPv6 addressing and transition mechanisms visualization
description: IPv6 is COMMON academic (Kurose 8e Ch 4, Tanenbaum 6e, InterviewBit) and an emerging network engineer topic. Add: IPv6 address format (128-bit, 8 groups of 4 hex digits), address types (unicast, multicast, anycast, link-local), IPv4 vs IPv6 header comparison (simplified IPv6 header), transition mechanisms (dual-stack, tunneling, NAT64).
acceptanceCriteria: IPv6 address format breakdown with abbreviated notation rules
IPv4 vs IPv6 header comparison showing removed fields
Address type visualization: unicast, multicast, anycast, link-local
Dual-stack, 6to4 tunneling, NAT64 transition mechanism demos
Why IPv6 was needed: IPv4 exhaustion timeline

title: Add mTLS (mutual TLS) handshake visualization for microservices
description: mTLS is #7 professional essential and standard for microservices security (AppViewX, The New Stack, buoyant.io). Service meshes automate it. Add: standard TLS (server-only auth) vs mTLS (both sides authenticate). Show mutual certificate exchange, client certificate validation, and service mesh automation (Istio/Linkerd sidecar injecting mTLS transparently).
acceptanceCriteria: Standard TLS vs mTLS side-by-side showing the additional client certificate step
Certificate chain visualization for both client and server
Service mesh mode: show sidecar proxy handling mTLS transparently
Step descriptions explain WHY mTLS matters for zero-trust microservices
Common failure modes: expired cert, untrusted CA, CN mismatch

title: Add circuit breaker, retry, and timeout resilience patterns visualization
description: Circuit breakers and retry patterns are COMMON in interview sources (DarkInterview, Hemaks) and essential for production systems. Add: circuit breaker state machine (closed → open → half-open), exponential backoff with jitter, timeout cascades, bulkhead isolation. Show how a failing downstream service propagates through a microservice chain with and without these patterns.
acceptanceCriteria: Circuit breaker state machine animation (closed → open → half-open)
Exponential backoff with jitter timing visualization
Cascade failure simulation: one service fails → all callers fail
Same scenario with circuit breaker showing graceful degradation
Configurable: failure threshold, recovery timeout, max retries

title: Add WebTransport protocol visualization (emerging QUIC-based alternative)
description: WebTransport has 75% browser support, is in Interop 2026 priorities, and is the emerging replacement for WebSocket in low-latency use cases. Add: QUIC-based transport showing reliable streams + unreliable datagrams, comparison with WebSocket (TCP-based, no unreliable mode), use cases (gaming, live video). Show Connection with datagrams and bidirectional streams.
acceptanceCriteria: WebTransport connection establishment over QUIC
Reliable stream vs unreliable datagram modes demonstrated
WebSocket vs WebTransport side-by-side comparison
Use case scenarios: gaming (unreliable datagrams), file transfer (reliable streams)
Browser support status and fallback strategy

title: Add ICMP, ping, and traceroute visualization for network debugging
description: ICMP is COMMON academic (Kurose 8e Ch 5) and essential for network debugging. Professional essential #8 (debugging tools). Add: ICMP echo request/reply (ping), traceroute hop-by-hop with TTL decrement, path MTU discovery, ICMP error messages (destination unreachable, time exceeded). Interactive traceroute where user sees each hop respond.
acceptanceCriteria: Ping simulation showing ICMP echo request/reply with RTT measurement
Traceroute animation showing TTL decrement at each router hop
ICMP error messages explained: destination unreachable, time exceeded
Path MTU discovery process
Interactive: user can trace route to different destinations

title: Add OAuth 2.0 / OIDC authentication flow visualization
description: OAuth 2.0 and JWT appear in Hemaks' modern HTTP stack and DarkInterview. Essential for any application with third-party auth. Add: Authorization Code flow (with PKCE), Client Credentials flow, Implicit flow (deprecated), and OIDC ID token. Show redirect chain, token exchange, and JWT structure (header.payload.signature).
acceptanceCriteria: Authorization Code + PKCE flow with redirect animation
Client Credentials flow for service-to-service auth
JWT structure breakdown: header, payload, signature with Base64 decode
Token refresh flow with access/refresh token lifecycle
Comparison: session-based auth vs token-based auth

title: Add eBPF networking overview (2026 trending, 300% YoY growth)
description: eBPF adoption grew 300% YoY. AWS EKS adopted Cilium (eBPF-based) as default CNI. Delivers 30-40% higher throughput than iptables. Add: high-level visualization of eBPF packet processing path vs traditional iptables, XDP (eXpress Data Path) for early packet processing, TC (Traffic Control) hooks. Show kernel-level interception without kernel module loading.
acceptanceCriteria: eBPF vs iptables packet processing path comparison
XDP processing at network driver level visualization
Performance comparison: throughput and latency improvement
Use cases: Cilium CNI, load balancing, DDoS mitigation
Conceptual (no actual eBPF code) — focus on architecture

title: Add VPN/tunneling visualization (IPsec, WireGuard)
description: VPN types appear in InterviewBit network engineer questions and are common professional knowledge. Add: IPsec tunnel mode vs transport mode, WireGuard (modern, simpler). Show packet encapsulation inside VPN tunnel, encryption envelope, and performance comparison. Contrast with Zero Trust (NET-072) as the modern alternative.
acceptanceCriteria: IPsec tunnel mode: original packet encapsulated inside encrypted outer packet
IPsec transport mode: only payload encrypted, original header visible
WireGuard: simplified handshake and modern crypto comparison
VPN vs ZTNA comparison for remote access
Performance: encryption overhead, latency impact

title: Add network flow (max flow / min cut) algorithm visualization
description: Network flow is a CORE competitive programming topic (cp-algorithms, TopCoder, VisuAlgo). Ford-Fulkerson, Edmonds-Karp, Dinic's algorithms. Bridges graph algorithms to networking (network capacity planning, transportation). Add interactive flow network with augmenting path animation and residual graph.
acceptanceCriteria: Interactive flow network graph with configurable edge capacities
Augmenting path animation for Ford-Fulkerson/Edmonds-Karp
Residual graph updates visible after each augmentation
Min-cut visualization showing the bottleneck edges
Step descriptions explain why each path was chosen

title: Add difficulty badges, prerequisite graph, and 6 learning paths to sidebar
description: Currently 9 protocols listed flat with no difficulty, prerequisites, or curriculum structure. Implement: (1) Difficulty badges (beginner/intermediate/advanced) for all topics, (2) Prerequisite links ('Learn TCP before TLS'), (3) 6 learning paths from curriculum design: University CS (12-week), FAANG Interview (4-week), Senior Engineer (2-week), Competitive Programming (8-week), Weekend Crash Course (2-day), AI/ML Engineer.
acceptanceCriteria: Every protocol topic has a difficulty badge in sidebar

title: Add real-world application context and interview tips to all existing topics
description: Existing 13 topics have good protocol mechanics but lack 'where is this used in production?' context and 'how does this come up in interviews?' guidance. For each existing topic, add: (1) Real-world applications section (e.g., TCP: 'PostgreSQL uses TCP for reliable query delivery'), (2) Interview tips (e.g., 'Google asks: why does YouTube use QUIC?'), (3) Common mistakes (e.g., 'Students confuse TCP sequence numbers with packet counts').
acceptanceCriteria: Each of 13 existing topics has 3+ real-world application examples
Each has 2+ interview tips with company attribution where possible

Content appears in properties panel under dedicated sections
Verified against research: real-world apps from Reddit/HN, tips from interview guides

title: Add cross-module links from networking topics to system-design and distributed modules
description: concept-module-map.ts already has TCP, DNS, HTTP, TLS, WebSocket entries. But missing: load balancing → system-design, congestion control → distributed, CDN → system-design, NAT → cloud architecture, QUIC → mobile design. Add comprehensive cross-module references for all new topics.
acceptanceCriteria: At least 15 cross-module links covering new and existing topics
